questions.question: Who led the Indian National Movement after 1915 and what was his approach?
questions.answer: Mahatma Gandhi led the movement, advocating non-violent civil disobedience and mass mobilization.

questions.question: When did Mahatma Gandhi return to India from South Africa and why was it significant?
questions.answer: He returned in 1915; his leadership brought new methods of mass movements against British rule.

questions.question: What was the Rowlatt Act and how did it affect Indians?
questions.answer: The Rowlatt Act allowed the government to arrest and detain people without trial, causing widespread anger and protests.

questions.question: Describe the Jallianwala Bagh massacre and its impact.
questions.answer: In 1919, General Dyer ordered firing on unarmed civilians at Jallianwala Bagh, killing hundreds; it intensified Indian anger against British rule.

questions.question: Explain the Non-Cooperation Movement and its objectives.
questions.answer: It was a mass movement led by Gandhi to boycott British institutions, promote self-reliance, and demand Swaraj (self-rule).

questions.question: Why was the Non-Cooperation Movement withdrawn?
questions.answer: It was withdrawn in 1922 due to the Chauri Chaura incident, where protesters killed police, contradicting Gandhi's principle of non-violence.

questions.question: What is Swaraj and how did Gandhi define it?
questions.answer: Swaraj means self-rule; Gandhi defined it as political independence as well as moral and social self-discipline.

questions.question: What was the Civil Disobedience Movement and when did it begin?
questions.answer: It began in 1930, led by Gandhi, and involved deliberately disobeying British laws, especially the salt laws, to assert Indian rights.

questions.question: Describe the Salt March and its significance.
questions.answer: Gandhi started the Dandi March from Sabarmati Ashram to produce salt illegally, highlighting British oppression and inspiring nationwide civil disobedience.

questions.question: What was the Khilafat Movement and why did it attract Indian Muslims?
questions.answer: It aimed to protect the Ottoman Caliphate; many Indian Muslims joined as a religious cause while supporting Indian nationalism.

questions.question: Which organization demanded separate electorates for Muslims and why?
questions.answer: The Muslim League demanded it to safeguard Muslim political rights under British rule.

questions.question: Explain the concept of Purna Swaraj and when it was declared.
questions.answer: Purna Swaraj means complete independence; it was declared at the Lahore Session in 1929.

questions.question: Who was the Viceroy during the Civil Disobedience Movement and what pact did he sign with Gandhi?
questions.answer: Lord Irwin; he signed the Gandhi-Irwin Pact in 1931 allowing some concessions to the Indian National Congress.

questions.question: What was the Simon Commission and why was it opposed?
questions.answer: A British committee to suggest constitutional reforms, opposed because it had no Indian members, sparking nationwide protests.

questions.question: Describe the Quit India Movement and its main demand.
questions.answer: Launched in 1942, it demanded immediate British withdrawal from India and involved mass protests and strikes.

questions.question: What role did boycotts play in the Indian National Movement?
questions.answer: Boycotts refused British goods and institutions, weakening colonial authority and promoting self-reliance.

questions.question: Explain the impact of the Jallianwala Bagh massacre on Indian nationalism.
questions.answer: It united Indians across communities against British oppression and strengthened the demand for self-rule.

questions.question: Why did Gandhi emphasize non-violence in all movements?
questions.answer: He believed moral high ground and mass participation without violence would expose British injustice and gain international support.

questions.question: Who were the key leaders of Indian nationalism besides Gandhi?
questions.answer: Leaders included Bal Gangadhar Tilak, Lala Lajpat Rai, and Bipin Chandra Pal, who advocated for political and social reform.

questions.question: How did the Civil Disobedience Movement affect British rule?
questions.answer: It disrupted British administration, inspired mass civil participation, and exposed the unsustainability of colonial control.

questions.question: From where did Gandhi start the Dandi March and why was it symbolic?
questions.answer: He started from Sabarmati Ashram; it symbolized defiance of unjust British laws and self-reliance.

questions.question: What was the main goal of the Indian National Congress in the 1920s and 1930s?
questions.answer: To achieve complete independence from British rule through mass movements and civil disobedience.

questions.question: Which incident intensified national protests against British rule in 1922?
questions.answer: The Chauri Chaura incident, where police were killed, leading Gandhi to withdraw the Non-Cooperation Movement.

questions.question: Explain the significance of Purna Swaraj declaration.
questions.answer: It marked the formal commitment of Indian leaders to complete independence and inspired civil disobedience campaigns nationwide.

questions.question: What was the overarching objective of Indian nationalist movements?
questions.answer: To free India from British colonial rule and establish self-governance based on democratic and ethical principles.
